summary: This bill would require covered platforms to design their services to prevent and mitigate harm to minors, provide safeguards for minors' data, prohibit them from conducting market or product research on those under 13 (under 17 with parental consent), and provide explanations and opt-outs for algorithms that use user-specific data. Enforcement would be conducted through the Federal Trade Commission (FTC) and state attorneys general in any federal district or state court. Covered platforms include "online platforms, video games, messaging applications, or video streaming services" which are used or likely to be used by people under 17.
background: This is the third Congress this bill has been introduced in, previously passing the Senate 91-3 in the 118th. Introduced after 2021 Facebook leak revealing it was aware of harmful societal effects from its platforms. Requires covered platforms to "exercise reasonable care" to “prevent and mitigate” a list of seven broad and vague "duty of care" provisions. No government entity can enforce the bill based on any speech protected by the First Amendment, but is primarily enforced by the FTC.

recommendation.reason: This bill is too vague about "harmful content" and could be used to target LGBTQIA+ people, censor speech protected under the First Amendment, and implement age verification systems.

summary: This bill would establish a federal-state partnership to eliminate tuition and required fees at all community colleges, two-year tribal colleges and universities, and for eligible students at public four-year institutions of higher education and private nonprofit historically Black colleges and universities; double the maximum Pell Grant award; allow Pell usage for living and non-tuition expenses; establish a $10 billion grant program to improve student outcomes at eligible institutions; and increase select funding by a factor of two or three. Pell Grants would be made mandatory spending, add annual inflation adjustments, increase lifetime eligibility to 7.5 years, be exempted from being taxed as income, and would be expanded to include Dreamers and students with other immigration statuses.
background: This is the sixth Congress this bill has been introduced in. First introduced in 2015 by Senator Sanders (I-VT). Introduced in this Senate by Sen. Sanders as S. 1832, co-sponsored by Sen. Adam Schiff (D-CA). Expands higher education access by making public colleges and universities tuition-free.

support: Radio and Machine Workers of America

recommendation.reason: College and university should be free, especially for the current 43 million Americans who have student loan debt and the future generations who will benefit from the increased opportunities.

bill.title: Understanding the True Cost of College Act of 2025

summary: This bill would require the Department of Education to create standard terminology and a standardized financial aid offer form with input from many stakeholders, including students. The form must contain estimated cost of attendance information, such as direct costs (e.g. tuition and fees, and housing and food when participating in a meal plan or similar), indirect costs (e.g. off-campus housing and food, transportation, and books); the covered academic period; the enrollment status; and whether tuition and fees are estimated or set. It must also include the aggregate amount of grants and scholarships that the student does not have to repay from a variety of sources, the net price, any recommended loans clearly marked as such with specified information, and additional information proscribed by the Secretary. All institutions of higher education receiving federal funds must use the form starting with the first award year after the Secretary finalizes the terminology and form.
background: This is the second Congress this bill has been introduced by Sen. Chuck Grassley (R-IA) and Rep. Young Kim. The Senate version, S. 1558, was gutted during markup in H.E.L.P. by chairman Sen. Bill Cassidy (R-LA) by removing the requirement to use a standard form. This bill is a better version of H.R. 6502 that has more organizational support.

support: The Institute for College Access & Success (TICAS)
The Institute for Hig her Education Policy (IHEP)
Student Senate for California Community Colleges
Los Angeles Area Chamber of Commerce
Partners for College Affordability and Public Trust
PHENOM (Public Higher Education Network of Massachusetts)

recommendation.reason: Financial aid forms should be easier to understand and standardized to enable students can truly compare the costs of college and make the best informed decision.

summary: This bill would reauthorize and expand the Federal Work-Study (FWS) program by increasing funding; prioritizing work-based learning; updating the allocation formula based on Pell Grant enrollment and student need; requires dedicating specified amounts to students with exceptional need, work-based learning, and community service; prevents using earned funds less than $2,500 for cost of attendance; and inform eligible students of SNAP benefits. It would also create a pilot grant program to build work-based learning positions; surveys for students with work-study, employers of students, and each institution that participates in FWS that are collected in a portal; a report to Congress every four years on the program and recommendations; and would require the GAO to conduct a study.
background: First introduced in 2019 and included in the 115th Congress’ Aim Higher Act, this bill has not received widespread organizational support. This bill would support the 6,948 students awarded work-study at UC San Diego in the 2022-23 academic year and increase the $21.5 million in FWS funds the University of California received for the year as of October 2025.

recommendation.reason: Reauthorizing and expanding the Federal Work-Study program would allow it to support more than the existing 600,000 students at over 3,400 campuses across the country.

summary: This bill would set the interest rates of all new and existing federal direct student loans to 2% on the unpaid principal balance. The bill would only apply to existing loans with an interest rate above 2%. A notice would be sent to borrowers with existing loans with the opportunity to opt-out within 90 days of receiving the notice. Borrowers with Federal Family Education Loan (FFEL) program loans would have the option to consolidate into the Direct Loan program to access the 2% rate. The Secretary of Education would be required to notify borrowers and loan servicers of the change and establish a complaint resolution process to address any errors or delays.
background: Existing law currently sets the fixed interest rates of federal direct loans annually through a formula that involves the high yield of 10-year Treasury notes at auction, an add-on percentage that varies depending on loan type, and whether the student is an undergraduate or graduate. Loans first disbursed between July 1, 2025 and July 1, 2026 carry interest rates between 6.39% and 8.94%. The average UC undergraduate who takes out loans has $17,200 of debt at graduation. In the 2024-25 academic year, 51,947 UC undergraduates (21.9%) had loans from the federal government with a total of $609,009,190 paid out.

support: National Association for College Admission Counseling (NACAC)
National Association of Secondary School Principals (NASSP)
National Association of Student Financial Aid Administrators (NASFAA)

recommendation.reason: Reducing the interest rate of student loans helps borrowers reduce long-term costs, pay down their principal faster, improve their financial stability, and potentially improve their credit.

bill.title: Connecting Communities Through Transit Planning Act of 2026

summary: This bill would make the Pilot Program for Transit-Oriented Development Planning permanent and convert it into a grant program. It would authorize $75 million per year for FY 2027–2031. It would clarify that TOD planning projects within new and existing fixed guideway systems, including bus rapid transit, qualify for funding. It would expand eligible activities to include predevelopment work, such as utility coordination, site evaluation, design, and outreach.
background: California transit agencies and metropolitan planning organizations have successfully used this pilot program to advance new housing projects near high-frequency rail and bus lines, improving access to transit while helping address the state’s housing shortage. For example, in the East Bay, construction is underway on a 97-unit apartment project for lower-income seniors next to BART's Lake Merritt station in Oakland’s Chinatown neighborhood, which was backed by a TOD planning grant to attract a private nonprofit developer and a mix of private and public funding sources for this project.

support: the East Bay Asian Local Development Corporation

recommendation.reason: Expanding TOD in the U.S., particularly in California, is critical for expanding housing supply and expanding transit use.
